ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for salted braised beehoon. FOODON_03302051 (rice noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GXM covers rice noodles as a category; no specific braised beehoon entry exists. Used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for salted braised beehoon or rice vermicelli dish at this specificity level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, representing a single-plate portion of salted braised beehoon as typically served at a Singapore coffee shop or casual restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Bee Hoon / Braised Bee Hoon — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists braised beehoon and fried beehoon entries. Values are adapted from the HPB braised beehoon entry (plain/seafood variant). The salted preparation increases sodium relative to the plain HPB entry; sodium is adjusted upward to reflect the salted preparation style. All other macros are taken directly from the HPB braised beehoon reference. Per-100g values are derived from the HPB per-serving data normalised to 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — rice noodles / rice vermicelli
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salted braised beehoon. GI of 53 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice vermicelli / rice noodles (GI range 53–58 in peer-reviewed literature; Atkinson et al. 2021, Diabetes Care). The lower bound of 53 is used as the dish is served in a light broth with seafood, which moderates glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 71.75) / 100 ≈ 38.0 g (serving carbohydrate = 20.5 g/100g × 350 g = 71.75 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg/100 g; ~1680 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid salted braised beehoon due to its high sodium content from soy sauce, salt, and broth; a single serving may contribute up to 70–84% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 53) and high glycemic load per serving (~38)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI of rice vermicelli is in the low-to-moderate range, the large carbohydrate load per serving (~72 g) produces a high glycemic load; people with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion size and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables or protein to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat content with seafood-derived cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content and moderate dietary cholesterol from seafood ingredients make salted braised beehoon a moderate concern for cardiovascular disease; individuals should limit portion size, request reduced-salt preparation, and balance with low-sodium meals throughout the day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salted Braised Beehoon is a Singaporean noodle dish made from thin rice vermicelli (beehoon) that is slow-braised in a savoury, lightly salted broth together with seafood such as prawns, squid, or fish slices, and aromatics including garlic, ginger, and spring onion. The dish is seasoned with soy sauce and salt, giving it a distinctly savoury and mildly briny flavour profile. The texture of the beehoon is soft and silky from absorbing the braising liquid, while the seafood contributes a tender, slightly chewy contrast. It is commonly served as a single-plate meal at Singapore coffee shops (kopitiams) and casual eateries. Nutritionally, salted braised beehoon is a moderate-energy dish (~118 kcal/100 g) with a carbohydrate-dominant macronutrient profile (approximately 20.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g), modest protein from seafood (~4.8 g/100 g), and low fat (~2.1 g/100 g). The primary clinical concern is its high sodium content (~480 mg/100 g), which is elevated due to the salted braising technique and soy-based seasoning; a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 1680 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low-to-moderate), but the glycemic load per serving is high (~38) due to the large carbohydrate portion, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is generally halal-suitable and dairy-free, though it contains common seafood allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in braised beehoon?
Is salted beehoon high in sodium?
What is the GI of rice vermicelli?
What seafood is in braised beehoon?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo braised beehoon entry, which is the closest approved primary source for this dish. The salted preparation variant is not listed as a distinct entry in HPB Foodo; sodium has been adjusted upward to reflect the salted style. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database rice vermicelli data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish category. Vitamin B6 (vitamin_b6_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace for this dish type per HPB source. The cuisine tag in the input JSON listed 'Filipino' but the dish as served at Lucky Cafe — North Canal Road in Singapore is a Singaporean kopitiam preparation; cuisine has been corrected to Singaporean. The input description 'Savory seafood beehoon in a light broth' is preserved in spirit but expanded for nutritional and clinical accuracy. GI source match type is analogous_estimate; no direct GI measurement for salted braised beehoon was found in the University of Sydney GI Database.
